cataloging_subject.label: Twenty-Six Political and Other Poems (Including 'Petty Job') from the Oxford MSS. Digby 102 and Douce 322
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Josef Kail's edition-defined twenty-six-work selection as encoded in APE9594.0001.001: all twenty-six poem divisions in the 1904 EETS Original Series 124 volume labeled Part I, printed pp. 1-149. Most front matter and the glossary/back matter survive only as page milestones and are not part of the encoded textual selection. This record does not describe one medieval poem, one authorial anthology, either whole manuscript, or a verified complete projected multi-part publication.

catalog_summary.title: Twenty-Six Political and Other Poems (Including 'Petty Job') from the Oxford MSS. Digby 102 and Douce 322
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English poets; the first twenty-four works are probably by one anonymous poet
catalog_summary.date: Digby sequence composed in the early fifteenth century; The Bird with Four Feathers circa 1390-1410; Pety Job mid-fifteenth century; selection published 1904
catalog_summary.region: England; witness dialect and proposed authorial locations vary by part and are not secure composition places

titles.value: Twenty-Six Political and Other Poems (Including 'Petty Job') from the Oxford MSS. Digby 102 and Douce 322
notes: Normalized capitalization follows the 1904 title page; Petty Job is retained only in the edition-level wrapper title.

titles.value: Twenty-six political and other poems (including Petty Job) from the Oxford MSS. Digby 102 and Douce 322

assertion.notes: The U-M bibliographic string is preserved as source-layer metadata; its D. J. Kail wording is not promoted to the editor's identity.

titles.value: Pety Job, or Parce mihi, Domine!

notes: The encoded Kail heading instead reads Here begynneth a tretyse of Parce michi, domine.

agents.name: Josef Kail
notes: Editor of the 1904 selection; current authority evidence supplies no verified birth or death date.

agents.name: Miss A. F. Parker
notes: Kail credits her with reading proofs against the manuscripts; the initials are not expanded without authority evidence.

agents.name: Richard Rolle of Hampole
notes: Recorded only to model the rejected manuscript attribution of Pety Job; this agent record is not an accepted authorship assertion.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English poets; the first twenty-four works are probably by one anonymous poet

assertion.notes: Aggregate catalog display for the 26-work anthology. It does not collapse the separately scoped anonymous Digby, Pety Job, and Bird authorships below.

scope.description: The first twenty-four separately headed works.

assertion.notes: Single authorship is strongly supported by coherence, style, and recurring language, but neither Kail's rejected abbot/prior profile nor Verheij's argued Westminster Benedictine/Chancery profile identifies the poet.
Three witness rubrics preserve the attribution, but modern scholarship rejects it on chronology, dialect, tone, and style; this record is attribution history, not an alternate accepted author.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous poet of The Bird with Four Feathers

scope.description: Proofs of the whole selection read against Digby 102 and Douce 322.
Headlines, some side-notes, punctuation and hyphens, and supervision of press sheets.

assertion.notes: Reviser is the closest controlled role; Furnivall is not treated as a blanket co-editor or medieval author.

scope.description: The 2006 OCR/TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 digital production layer.

assertion.notes: The metadata role vocabulary has no encoder or digitizer value; lineage carries the digital-production facts.

date_statements.display: Early fifteenth century, probably first quarter

assertion.notes: The broad range preserves uncertainty. Kail's individual dates are edition-layer proposals, and the last six works lack datable events.
Douce 322's 1450-1475 production range is not substituted for the work-composition date.
PCMEP's circa 1400 is retained as a suggestion within the broader sourced range.

places.label: Malvern area
notes: Southwestern Worcestershire-border scribal dialect localization for Digby 102, not a proved place of composition.

scope.description: Scribal dialect of the Digby 102 witness text.

assertion.notes: This witness-level dialect localization must not be read as the anonymous poet's place of composition.
Dialect classification, not a secure place of composition.
PCMEP cautiously infers a London-area origin from the concentration of complete witnesses; this is not confirmed composition evidence.

scope.description: Latin lesson headings, subjects, liturgical phrases, and/or recurring refrains intrinsic to parts 24-26.

assertion.notes: The first twenty-three Digby poems contain no Latin according to Verheij. Modern English editorial apparatus is not a work-level discovery language.

form_statements.description: All twenty-six count-once works are verse. Two prose-like Pety Job colophon statements are encoded as L elements but do not make the edited selection mixed-form.

subject_statements.label: Governance, kingship, commons, justice, and clerical reform
Death, penitence, salvation, and the body-soul relationship
The Office of the Dead and the Book of Job
Youth, beauty, strength, riches, transitoriness, and memento mori

summaries.value: Josef Kail's 1904 edition-defined selection of twenty-six anonymous Middle English verse works: twenty-four political, moral, and devotional poems from Bodleian Library MS Digby 102, followed by Pety Job and The Bird with Four Feathers from MS Douce 322. The final three works draw on the Office of the Dead and Job, while the wider Digby sequence ranges across governance, reform, ethical counsel, Eucharistic devotion, death, and salvation.

The record counts the twenty-six separately headed poems once. The ten lessons nested inside poem XXIV are non-counting analytical sections. The 1904 title-page Part I wording is retained, but neither a published Part II nor the projected publication's completion or abandonment has been verified. Modern editorial prose and apparatus remain edition-layer material rather than additional medieval works or work-level English-language facets.

extent.description: Thirty-six content-part records comprise twenty-six count-bearing verse works and ten null-count analytical lesson sections nested inside poem XXIV. The XML contains 529 LG elements and 4,696 raw L elements; the verse-line total is 4,694 after excluding two prose-like Pety Job colophon statements encoded as L. Poem XXIV has 418 L elements against the edition's idealized 52 eight-line stanzas/416 lines, Pety Job has 684 poetic lines plus the two colophon statements, and the Bird poem has 240 raw lines with an encoded note that print line number 222 repeats after 223.

content_parts.label: I. Loue god, and drede.
content_parts.summary: Political and moral counsel to governors on justice, charity, self-knowledge, and the fear and love of God.
notes: Kail supplies A.D. 1400; treat as an edition-layer dating claim.

content_parts.label: II. Mede and muche thank.
content_parts.summary: A debate contrasting true service and earned thanks with flattery, patronage, and material reward.
notes: Dialogue speakers must not be mistaken for named authors.

content_parts.label: III. Treuth, reste, and pes.
content_parts.summary: Political and ethical counsel urging truth, stability, and peace amid failures of governance.
notes: Kail supplies early 1401; retain as an edition-layer claim.

content_parts.label: IV. Lerne say wele, say litel, or say noȝt.
content_parts.summary: Didactic counsel on prudent speech, restraint, and the moral and political dangers of ill-advised words.
notes: Exact XML heading retained.

content_parts.label: V. Wyt & Wille.
content_parts.summary: An ethical treatment of reason and will, counseling resistance to corrupt desire and bad counsel.
notes: Ampersand is present in the encoded heading.

content_parts.label: VI. To lyue bodyly, is perylous.
content_parts.summary: A moral exhortation contrasting perilous bodily and worldly living with spiritually governed conduct.
notes: The XML heading contains an intrusive OCR/read-reference string; the shortened display label is supplied.

content_parts.label: VII. Man, know thy self, and lerne to dye.
content_parts.summary: A memento-mori exhortation joining self-knowledge, discipline of the soul, and preparation for death.
notes: DIMEV association is supported by incipit/locus matching; current catalog gives the physical span.

content_parts.label: VIII. A good makynge of iour delaye.
content_parts.summary: A topical complaint against clerical delay and failure, framed as counsel toward timely reform.
notes: Kail labels it against the clergy and dates it 1408-09; keep both source-attributed.

content_parts.label: IX. With god of loue & pes, ȝe trete.
content_parts.summary: An Easter exhortation that links reconciliation with God, moral renewal, love, and public peace.
notes: Kail supplies Easter 1410.

content_parts.label: X. A good steryng to heuenward.
content_parts.summary: Spiritual counsel redirecting the reader from worldly error and presumption toward heaven.
notes: Fifteen fourteen-line stanzas.

content_parts.label: XI. God & man ben made atte on.
content_parts.summary: An Easter devotional poem on Christ's joining of divinity and humanity and the believer's reconciliation with God.
notes: Kail supplies Easter 1412.

content_parts.label: XII. God saue the kyng, and kepe the croun.
content_parts.summary: Prayer and public counsel for the king, crown, common good, and righteous governance.
notes: Kail supplies Easter 1413.

content_parts.label: XIII. Dede is worchyng.
content_parts.summary: A two-part political and moral poem that tests professions of good governance against actions and consequences.
notes: Kail labels the work A.D. 1414 and in two parts.

content_parts.label: XIV. Man, be warre er the be woo.
content_parts.summary: Admonition that higher rank brings greater responsibility and that prudent reform must precede disaster.
notes: Kail supplies early 1418.

content_parts.label: XV. The descryuyng of mannes membres.
content_parts.summary: A body-part meditation that turns human anatomy into moral instruction and memento mori.
notes: The catalog/DIMEV span is more reliable than the first internal milestone, which occurs after opening lines.

content_parts.label: XVI. A remembraunce of LIJ folyes.
content_parts.summary: A catalog of fifty-two follies combining moral rebuke with topical political and social criticism.
notes: Nine fourteen-line stanzas; Kail supplies A.D. 1419.

content_parts.label: XVII. Loue that god loueth.
content_parts.summary: Instruction in charity and the right ordering of love toward God, neighbor, and virtuous action.
notes: Exact title retained from the edition/XML.

content_parts.label: XVIII. The declaryng of religioun.
content_parts.summary: A reformist explanation of true religious life and the conduct expected of clergy and professed religious.
notes: Kail supplies A.D. 1421; DIMEV association is incipit/locus supported.

content_parts.label: XIX. God's Appeal to Man.
content_parts.summary: A complaint in which God addresses sinful humanity and calls it to recognition, amendment, and gratitude.
notes: The XML's internal folio milestone `110a` is an error; the current catalog and sequence establish ff. 119v-120r.

content_parts.label: XX. How Man's flesh complained to God against Christ.
content_parts.summary: A theological complaint and dialogue that dramatizes human embodiment, sin, divine justice, and Christ's claims upon humanity.
notes: Bracketed title is editorially supplied in Kail.

content_parts.label: XXI. A lernyng to good leuynge.
content_parts.summary: Practical moral instruction for virtuous living, repentance, charity, and responsibility toward others.
notes: Twenty eight-line stanzas.

content_parts.label: XXII. Knowe thy self and thy god.
content_parts.summary: A compact devotional meditation joining self-knowledge, knowledge of God, humility, and salvation.
notes: Nine eight-line stanzas.

content_parts.label: XXIII. Of the sacrament of the Altere.
content_parts.summary: A Eucharistic devotional poem on Christ's presence in the sacrament of the altar and the communicant's response.
notes: Sixteen eight-line stanzas.

content_parts.label: XXIV. The Lessouns of the Dirige.
content_parts.summary: A Middle English verse paraphrase of the lessons of Matins in the Office of the Dead, organized as nine lessons plus Libera me.
notes: The edition asserts 52 eight-line stanzas = 416 lines, but raw XML has 418 L elements; ten child lessons are non-counting analytical sections.

content_parts.label: Lectio prima: Parce mihi, domine.
content_parts.summary: First lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.

content_parts.label: Lectio secunda: Tedet animam meam.
content_parts.summary: Second lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.

content_parts.label: Lectio tertia: Manus tue fecerunt me.
content_parts.summary: Third lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.

content_parts.label: Lectio quarta: Quantas habeo.
content_parts.summary: Fourth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.

content_parts.label: Lectio quinta: Homo natus.
content_parts.summary: Fifth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.

content_parts.label: Lectio sexta: Quis mihi hoc tribuat.
content_parts.summary: Sixth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.

content_parts.label: Lectio septima: Spiritus meus.
content_parts.summary: Seventh lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision; XML records an abbreviation note in the heading.

content_parts.label: Lectio octava: Pelli mee.
content_parts.summary: Eighth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.

content_parts.label: Lectio nona: Quare de uulua eduxisti me.
content_parts.summary: Ninth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.
notes: Non-counting subdivision; the XML heading records a manuscript-reading note.

content_parts.label: Libera me, domine.
content_parts.summary: Concluding Libera me section following the nine lessons.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.

content_parts.label: XXV. Pety Job, or Parce mihi, Domine!
content_parts.summary: A mid-fifteenth-century penitential monologue that expands and paraphrases the nine lessons of the Office of the Dead from Job, with Latin subjects and the recurring plea Parce mihi, domine.
notes: 684 poetic lines plus two prose-like colophon statements encoded as L. Direct f.15r image and Fein/Kail conflict with DIMEV 3075's printed endpoint ff.10ra-15vb. Richard Rolle attribution rejected.

content_parts.label: XXVI. The Bird with Four Feathers.
content_parts.summary: A penitential allegory in which a narrator encounters a plucked bird whose lost feathers are youth, beauty, strength, and riches; variable-length stanzas return to Parce mihi, domine.

locators.value: ff. 15r-16v; begins after Pety Job on f. 15r

notes: Common modern title supplied from scholarship; the XML heading reads `Here begynneth a tretyse of Parce michi, domine.` DIMEV gives ff.15rb-16vb.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned APE9594 XML at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Repository identity, U-M identifier, Kail source edition, titles, twenty-six-work structure, line/group counts, page milestones, notes, and OCR/editorial statement.

evidence.citation: Josef Kail, Twenty-Six Political and Other Poems (Including 'Petty Job') from the Oxford MSS. Digby 102 and Douce 322, EETS Original Series 124, Part I (1904), Toronto scan.
evidence.summary: Title page, Part I statement, editorial responsibilities, introduction, all twenty-six poems on printed pp. 1-149, glossary, and exact edition/page scope.
evidence.locator: PDF pp. 17, 21-37, 39-187, and 189-247; target poems printed pp. 1-149

evidence.citation: GND authority record 1157837611 for Kail, Josef.
evidence.summary: Preferred name, GND and VIAF identifiers, and association with the target publication; no birth or death date supplied.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries medieval manuscripts catalog, MS Digby 102, pinned catalog XML.
evidence.summary: Current shelfmark, 142 parchment leaves, London 1425-1450 production, Brewers' Guild clerk scribe, and target sequence ff. 98r-127v.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries medieval manuscripts catalog, MS Douce 322, pinned catalog XML.
evidence.summary: Current shelfmark, parchment support, England 1450-1475 production, Dartford association, and catalog context for parts 25-26; inherited Richard Rolle attribution remains source history only.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 3075, Pety Job.
evidence.summary: Title, incipit, 57-stanza structure, Latin features, five-witness census, and the source-specific conflicting Douce endpoint ff. 10ra-15vb.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 924, The Bird with Four Feathers.
evidence.summary: Title, incipit, witness census, and complete, altered/abridged, incomplete, and fragmentary witness distinctions.

evidence.citation: PCMEP record for The Bird with Four Feathers.
evidence.summary: Penitential genre, transitoriness and chanson d'aventure classifications, East Midlands composition dialect, 1390-1410 date, tentative London-area reasoning, and source-specific 241-line count.

evidence.citation: Susanna Greer Fein, Moral Love Songs and Laments (1998), METS digital edition.
evidence.summary: Modern edition and study of Pety Job and The Bird with Four Feathers, their authorship, language, dates, loci, witnesses, forms, and the visible Douce f. 15r transition.
evidence.locator: Bird PDF pp. 256-289; Pety Job PDF pp. 290-360

evidence.citation: Louis J. P. Verheij, Where of is mad al mankynde: The Middle English Digby Poems (doctoral thesis, 2009).
evidence.summary: Complete modern edition of the twenty-four Digby poems, broad chronology, scribal Malvern-area dialect localization, and anonymous-author analysis.

evidence.citation: Louis J. P. Verheij, New Light on the Author of the Twenty-Four Poems in Oxford, Bodleian Library, Digby 102 (2012).
evidence.summary: Rejects Kail's abbot/prior profile and argues, without identifying the poet, for a Westminster Benedictine and Chancery-clerk profile.

evidence.citation: Cathy Hume, The Life of Job: Translation, Poem or Play? (2018), accepted manuscript.
evidence.summary: Pety Job textual and liturgical family, Latin-English presentation, and short quotation of lines 1-8.

evidence.citation: CUDL/Scriptorium TEI description for Trinity College Cambridge MS O.9.38.
evidence.summary: msItem 21 gives Bird locus ff. 24r-25r, Middle English main language, Latin title, and incipit/explicit boundaries.

open_questions.question: Was a Part II ever published or definitively abandoned?
next_steps: Seek positive bibliographic evidence before describing the 1904 Part I scan as a complete projected multi-part publication.

open_questions.question: What are Josef Kail's authoritative full life dates?
next_steps: Resolve the editor's identity and death date from authoritative biographical evidence before making a worldwide copyright-term conclusion.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous Digby poet be identified beyond the competing source-attributed profiles?
next_steps: Keep Kail's rejected profile and Verheij's argued Westminster Benedictine/Chancery profile distinct from identity and authorship.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV 3075 print a Douce endpoint of ff. 10ra-15vb when the direct f. 15r image, Fein, Kail, and the next work's opening support an ending on f. 15r?
next_steps: Preserve both source claims and seek repository or DIMEV clarification; do not invent a second foliation or silently repair the record.

open_questions.question: How should PCMEP's 241-line Bird count relate to Kail/XML's 240-line representation?
next_steps: Collate the PCMEP/Brown base text before changing the repository extent.

notes: Catalog the edition-defined twenty-six-work selection, not one poem or either whole codex.
Count each top-level poem once; the ten nested lessons of poem XXIV are analytical sections with null item counts.
Keep modern English editorial prose outside work-level language facets; expose Latin only for parts 24-26.
Treat Digby 102's Malvern-area dialect as a witness-level scribal localization, not a composition place.
Keep the Richard Rolle witness-rubric attribution as rejected attribution history.
Retain Part I on the edition layer without claiming a published Part II or a complete projected multi-part edition.
Preserve the DIMEV 3075 Douce endpoint conflict and the exact shared f. 15r seam between Pety Job and The Bird with Four Feathers.
Use 4,694 as the audited poetic-line total while retaining 4,696 raw XML L elements and both Pety Job colophon statements.
Do not transfer manuscript production dates, edition publication place, or holding locations into work-composition claims.
